name: Migrate to Strict TypeScript Incrementally
description: Enable strict mode in an existing TypeScript project one flag at a time to avoid a massive migration and catch type errors gradually.
trendingReason: Teams adopting strict TypeScript report 20-40% fewer runtime bugs in production

steps.title: Check current strictness level
steps.description: Review your tsconfig.json to see which strict flags are already enabled.

steps.title: Enable strict flags one at a time
steps.description: Instead of enabling strict: true all at once, enable individual flags and fix errors incrementally.
steps.tip: Recommended order: noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, strictFunctionTypes, strictBindCallApply, noImplicitThis, alwaysStrict.

steps.title: Count errors for each strict flag
steps.description: Measure the scope of each flag before committing to fix it.
steps.command: echo "noImplicitAny: $(npx tsc --noEmit --noImplicitAny 2>&1 | grep -c 'error TS')" && echo "strictNullChecks: $(npx tsc --noEmit --strictNullChecks 2>&1 | grep -c 'error TS')"

steps.title: Enable strictNullChecks and fix null errors
steps.description: This is typically the most impactful flag. Fix undefined access patterns with optional chaining and nullish coalescing.
steps.command: npx tsc --noEmit --strictNullChecks 2>&1 | grep 'error TS' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
steps.tip: Use optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) instead of non-null assertions (!) wherever possible.
steps.warning: Non-null assertions (!) silence the compiler but do not fix the underlying issue. Prefer proper null checks.

steps.title: Enable full strict mode
steps.description: Once all individual flags pass, enable strict: true to lock in all current and future strict checks.
steps.command: npx json -I -f tsconfig.json -e 'this.compilerOptions.strict = true' && npx tsc --noEmit
steps.tip: After enabling strict: true, you can remove the individual flags since strict enables all of them.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Incremental Strict TypeScript Migration
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Current TypeScript Errors ==="
CURRENT=$(npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | grep -c 'error TS' || true)
echo "Current errors: $CURRENT"

echo ""
echo "=== Error Count Per Strict Flag ==="
for flag in noImplicitAny strictNullChecks strictFunctionTypes strictBindCallApply noImplicitThis alwaysStrict; do
  COUNT=$(npx tsc --noEmit --$flag 2>&1 | grep -c 'error TS' || true)
  echo "$flag: $COUNT errors"
done

echo ""
echo "=== Recommended Migration Order ==="
echo "1. alwaysStrict        (usually zero errors)"
echo "2. noImplicitThis      (rare in modern React/Node)"
echo "3. strictBindCallApply (low impact)"
echo "4. strictFunctionTypes (moderate impact)"
echo "5. noImplicitAny       (high impact, most common)"
echo "6. strictNullChecks    (highest impact, most valuable)"

faq.question: Can I enable strict mode for new files only?
faq.answer: Yes. Use a tsconfig.strict.json that extends your base config with strict: true, and apply it to new directories via project references. Or use // @ts-strict-mode comments with ts-strict-plugin.

faq.question: Is strict: true the strictest setting?
faq.answer: No. strict: true enables a set of flags but there are additional strict-ish options like noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes, and noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature that provide even more type safety.

faq.question: How do I handle third-party libraries without strict types?
faq.answer: Create declaration files (.d.ts) to patch incomplete types, or use module augmentation. For untyped packages, install @types/package-name if available, or create a minimal declaration with 'declare module "package-name";'.
